title: About
text: I’m naturally curious and learn by doing. Data science fits because it sits at the intersection of the qualitative and the quantitative, and only lands when it’s tied to the people acting on it. My background reflects that: psychology at UCSB, a math post-bacc at CUNY, the rest picked up on the job.

title: About
text:
Starting in Support
My first exposure to business data was support at ZocDoc. After enough conversations the same issues cluster in ways that aren’t always visible upstream. I started pulling ticket data into spreadsheets and sharing it with product — the habit stuck: start with what’s actually happening, then figure out what to do.

title: About
text:
Product Specialist at Square
I joined Square as a product specialist during the swipe-to-chip-to-contactless transition, bridging support and product. Over five years I grew into an analytics role. I built a classifier that automated a big chunk of support case tagging and freed up real CS capacity. Mostly, though, Square is where I learned how much of the value sits at the seam between functions.
In 2017 I also taught a data analytics course at General Assembly — 10 weeks of SQL, stats, and experimentation. Teaching is the fastest way to find out whether you actually understand something.

title: About
text:
Building DS Chops at Atlassian
Joined Atlassian in 2020 as the first data scientist hire for the events team — tight enough scope to build the pipeline end-to-end instead of inheriting one. From there, moved into marketing DS: ICP modeling, buying group analysis, and propensity scoring for the enterprise GTM org, partnering closely with Marketing Ops and sales leadership. Same seam-between-functions work as Square, now with modeling tools behind it.
Toward the end I spent a lot of time on how LLMs change the shape of DS work. They’re a real unlock when paired with verification — checking the join, sanity-checking the numbers, pressure-testing the framing before it reaches a stakeholder. I’ve seen the tooling work well, and I’ve seen it produce work that looks right and isn’t. A lot of my last six months there was helping the data org find that balance — MCP connections, knowledge files, walking people through their first workflows. That pulled on the same thing I liked about teaching.

title: About
text:
Where I Am Now
Based in the SF Bay Area, looking for senior DS roles where the work is close to the business and I can help a team spot the high-leverage AI use cases, build them, and ship them.

title: About
text:
Education

B.A. Psychology — UC Santa Barbara
Post-Baccalaureate, Mathematics — City University of New York

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
text: View on GitHub Live App

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
text:
What This Project Does
Open your banking app and look at a recent purchase. Behind the scenes, your bank received something ugly and cryptic, like:

SQ *BLUE BOTTLE COFFEE OAKLAND CA

A system has to turn that into something a person can read: the merchant is Blue Bottle Coffee, the category is Coffee, and it’s not a recurring subscription. That cleanup step is called enrichment, and thousands of finance apps depend on it being right.
Here’s the hard part. It’s fairly easy to build something that attempts this. It’s genuinely difficult to know how good your attempt actually is — because “correct” is fuzzy (is Blue Bottle as good as Blue Bottle Coffee?), and because the complaints you get are vague (“your categories are wrong for my users”). You can’t eyeball quality across millions of transactions.
This project isn’t an enrichment system. It’s the measuring tape for one. It’s a harness that tells you, in a principled way, exactly where an enrichment system is wrong, which failures matter most, and what to fix first.

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
section: Why This Matters in the Real World
text:
Why This Matters in the Real World
Plenty of data science projects build a model and report a single accuracy number. But in production, the harder and more valuable job is often the evaluation: defining what “good” means, turning a fuzzy customer complaint into a specific engineering fix, and catching the moment a “better” model quietly breaks something it used to get right.
This project recreates that evaluation discipline end-to-end. It scores a deliberately simple enrichment system so the spotlight stays on the measurement, not the model.






Note



A note on the data. The transaction strings here are synthetic descriptors for real merchants — made-up strings formatted to look like real bank statements, never actual cardholder data (which is private and contains personal information). So the specific numbers below are a demonstration of the method, not a production benchmark. The point is the approach, which carries over directly to real data. The category labels use Plaid’s public Personal Finance Category taxonomy (16 broad categories, 103 detailed ones).

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
text:
The Approach in Plain English
I built the project in layers, each answering a question the layer before it raised.
1. Define “correct” before measuring it. I wrote a labeling rulebook that settles the judgment calls up front: Starbucks is Coffee, not Fast Food; Amazon is an online marketplace, not a superstore; when a delivery app like DoorDash hides the real restaurant, you label the app and admit the restaurant is unknowable. Then I hand-built a 52-transaction answer key covering the messy patterns that trip systems up — payment-processor prefixes (SQ *, PYPL *), delivery apps that mask the merchant, ALL-CAPS truncation, and store-ID noise.
2. Score two versions of the system. A first-pass enricher (v1) and an improved one (v2), so I could measure not just quality but change in quality.
3. Handle the “close enough” problem with a judge — and then check the judge. Strict text-matching would wrongly mark Blue Bottle as a miss against Blue Bottle Coffee. So a judge decides when two names mean the same merchant. Crucially, I don’t trust a judge I haven’t measured: I validated it against 24 human-labeled pairs, including deliberately tricky ones (is Amazon the same as Amazon Prime? No). The judge agreed with human judgment 92% of the time.
4. Turn a vague complaint into a ranked to-do list. This is the centerpiece. You type a real complaint — “categorization is wrong for a lot of my users” — and the harness finds the failing transactions, groups them by root cause, and ranks the groups by how much damage they do.
5. Catch silent regressions. Every time the model changes, the harness re-checks every transaction and flags any that used to be right and just became wrong.

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
text:
What the Harness Found
Moving from v1 to v2 improved every headline number — merchant accuracy jumped from 51% to 82%, category accuracy from 62% to 91%. But the averages hide the interesting parts.



Quality improved across the board from v1 to v2. Synthetic data; methodology demo, not production metrics.


Finding 1 — Averages lie; quality is wildly uneven. Clean strings get enriched nearly perfectly. Transactions where a delivery app or payment processor masks the real merchant are where everything falls apart. A single accuracy number would have hidden that completely.



The same system is excellent on some patterns and poor on others. Red bars are the segments to fix. Synthetic data; methodology demo.


Finding 2 — The system doesn’t know when it’s wrong. A confidence score is only useful if it tracks reality. It doesn’t here, and unevenly: the guess-from-a-keyword path reports 40% confidence but is 0% accurate (confidently wrong, which is the worst kind — those answers look trustworthy and reach customers), while the delivery-app path is the opposite, underconfident. You only see this by comparing confidence to accuracy along each path, not in aggregate.



Stated confidence vs. actual accuracy. Gaps in either direction are mis-calibration. Synthetic data; methodology demo.


Finding 3 — “Better on average” can still break things. v2 improved 18 merchants but broke one: its smart new rule for delivery apps saw the PYPL (PayPal) prefix on a Steam game purchase and mislabeled it as PayPal, when v1 had correctly called it Steam. The rule that fixed many transactions quietly broke this one. This is the entire reason you run an evaluation on every change — a release that improves the average can still ship a specific, customer-visible regression.
Finding 4 — Some things can’t be known from one transaction. Recurring-subscription detection is the one thing here I don’t report as a headline score, on purpose. Whether a charge is part of a subscription is a fact about a series of transactions, so a system — or an evaluation — that sees one line at a time can’t honestly measure it. The harness keeps recurring as a failure-mode demonstration instead: a real gym membership with no telltale word gets missed, and a one-off purchase at a store named “Monthly Market” wrongly trips the flag because of the word monthly. (An earlier draft scored a perfect 1.0 here. That was an artifact of a tiny answer key where the labels and the detector keyed on the same words — and honestly, any number I’d quote would mostly reflect how many hard cases I chose to include, not real performance.) The real fix isn’t a cleverer string rule; it’s looking at transaction history.

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
section: The Complaint Investigator (the part I’m most proud of)
text:
The Complaint Investigator (the part I’m most proud of)
When a customer says “categorization is wrong for a lot of my users,” that’s unactionable. The harness turns it into this:









Rank
Root cause
Share of errors
Was confident while wrong?




1
Merchant not recognized at all (coverage gap)
67%
No (low confidence)


2
Delivery app masks the merchant
17%
Yes


3
Payment-processor prefix
17%
Yes



…plus a specific recommendation for each: close the knowledge-base coverage gap, stop guessing the masked merchant and flag low confidence instead, extend the prefix-stripping rules. That’s the difference between “quality is bad” and “here are the three things to fix, in order.”

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
section: What I’d Do Differently in Production
text:
What I’d Do Differently in Production

Build the answer key from real descriptors, with two people labeling a sample and measuring how often they agree — the synthetic seed here is a stand-in for that.
Use the confidence calibration as a release gate — suppress or human-review the confidently-wrong paths instead of showing them to users.
Add the regression check to CI so no model change ships without passing the per-transaction diff.
Bring in transaction history for recurring detection — you can’t always tell a subscription from a single line of text; you need to see the pattern over time.

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
text:
How It’s Built
Deliberately lightweight and fully reproducible. The whole thing runs offline for free, and the harness is model-agnostic — the same scoring, investigation, and regression layers work whether the enricher is a simple rules engine or a large language model, which you can swap in with one setting.

title: EnrichEval — A Quality Lab for Transaction Enrichment
text:
Stack
Python | pandas | scikit-learn | Matplotlib | Streamlit | Anthropic API (optional backend)

title: Support Ticket Classification
text: View on GitHub Live App

title: Support Ticket Classification
text:
What This Project Does
When a support ticket arrives, it needs to reach the right team fast. But the right classification method depends on how much labeled data you have. A startup with 500 tickets needs a fundamentally different approach than a platform processing millions.
This project compares seven classification approaches — from TF-IDF baselines to fine-tuned LLMs — across data volumes from 100 to ~10K labeled tickets, using 12,091 synthetic tickets modeled after a real AI-product help center taxonomy. The key output is a learning curve analysis showing where each method’s advantage kicks in.

title: Support Ticket Classification
text:
Why This Matters
Most ML classification comparisons train on a fixed dataset and report a single accuracy number. But in practice, the amount of labeled data you have is the primary constraint — and it changes which method you should use.
The fine-tuned LLM dominates from just 500 labeled tickets onward (88% F1), beating every classical method trained on the full dataset. At higher volumes the gap only widens. The only reason to choose a classical method is infrastructure cost — fine-tuning requires API access or GPU, while embeddings+XGBoost runs on a CPU. At a company with internal LLM infrastructure, fine-tuning is the obvious default.

title: Support Ticket Classification
text:
What’s Inside
The app has 13 pages organized around method comparison:
Data & Methods:

Data Explorer — Browse 12,091 synthetic tickets across 12 categories, see class distributions and example tickets
TF-IDF + Logistic / XGBoost — The baseline every project should start with
Embeddings + XGBoost — Sentence embeddings as features, with structured metadata (customer tier, account age)
Fine-Tuned BERT — End-to-end fine-tuning on ticket text
LLM Zero/Few-Shot — Prompt engineering with no training data
Fine-Tuned LLM — Fine-tuning GPT-4o-mini for classification
Distillation — Using o1-mini as a teacher model to label data for GPT-4o-mini

Analysis:

Volume vs. Method — Learning curves from 100 to ~10K tickets with real measured data at every point
Error Analysis — What each method gets wrong: category boundary confusion, semantic overlap, rare categories
Production Architecture — Cascade system: XGBoost handles confident classifications, fine-tuned LLM handles the low-confidence tail
Methodology — Data generation, evaluation protocol, assumptions
Glossary — Plain-language definitions of every method and concept

title: Support Ticket Classification
text:
Key Finding








Data Volume
Recommended Method
Why




< 500 tickets
LLM Few-Shot
Not enough data to train. Prompt engineering gets ~78% F1 with zero labeled examples.


500+ tickets
Fine-Tuned LLM
Dominates on accuracy (88-96% F1). Requires API access or GPU. Cost-constrained alternative: Embeddings+XGBoost.



At a company with internal LLM infrastructure, the cost constraint for fine-tuned LLM inference drops to near-zero, making it the default choice at any volume where you have enough data.

title: Support Ticket Classification
text:
The Data
12,091 unique synthetic support tickets generated via Claude Haiku, across 12 categories modeled after a real AI-product public help center:








Category
Share
Description




Billing & Payments
15.2%
Refunds, charges, invoices, credits


ChatGPT Product
14.8%
Features, errors, image generation, memory, search


Account Access
13.6%
Login issues, password reset, phone verification


API Errors
11.8%
Rate limits, 429 errors, latency, error codes


ChatGPT Apps
8.3%
iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, connected apps


Account Management
7.6%
Members, orgs, projects, email changes


API Usage
7.1%
How-to, getting started, best practices


Enterprise
6.4%
SSO, SCIM, workspace setup, admin


GPTs
5.3%
Custom GPT creation, publishing, actions


Newer Products
5.0%
Sora, Codex, ChatGPT Atlas


Privacy & Policy
3.4%
Data privacy, training opt-out, exports


Security
1.5%
API key safety, vulnerability reporting



Natural category overlap exists between semantically similar classes (e.g., API Usage vs. API Errors, Account Access vs. Account Management), which is where methods diverge most in performance.

title: Support Ticket Classification
text:
The Approach

Data Generation (Python)
12,091 unique tickets generated using Claude Haiku with structured prompts specifying category, customer tier, urgency, and account metadata. Each ticket is a unique natural-language formulation with realistic class imbalance.


Model Training & Evaluation
All supervised methods trained on the same 80/20 stratified split (~9,557 train / ~2,390 test). Learning curves measured at 100, 500, 1K, 2.5K, 5K, and ~9.5K tickets. All reported F1 scores are real measured values — no projected or simulated numbers.


Interactive App (Next.js)
Same stack as the Marketing Measurement project: Next.js, TypeScript, Recharts for visualization, Tailwind + shadcn/ui for the interface. Interactive learning curve chart lets you toggle methods on/off and see exactly where each method’s advantage kicks in.

title: Support Ticket Classification
section: What I’d Do Differently in Production
text:
What I’d Do Differently in Production

Active learning — Use model uncertainty to prioritize which tickets to label next, reducing labeling cost
Hierarchical classification — Tier 1 (broad routing) then Tier 2 (specific workflow) as separate models
Drift detection — Monitor for new ticket types that don’t fit existing categories
Human-in-the-loop — Route low-confidence classifications to human agents, use their decisions as training data
A/B testing the classifier — Compare routing accuracy and resolution time between methods in production

title: Support Ticket Classification
text:
Stack
Python | Next.js | TypeScript | Recharts | Tailwind | shadcn/ui | Vercel

title: InterviewReady
text: Live App

title: InterviewReady
text:
What This Project Does
Paste a job description URL and InterviewReady generates a tailored practice session — technical coding questions, case studies, and behavioral questions calibrated to the specific role, company, and seniority level. Answer in-app and get immediate AI-evaluated feedback with scoring and coaching.
It’s the tool I wanted when I was prepping for interviews and couldn’t find: something that adapts to the actual job instead of recycling the same generic question bank.

title: InterviewReady
text:
How It Works

Setup
Paste a job posting URL (or the raw text). The app auto-extracts the company name, role title, seniority level, and technical skills from the JD. Pick an interview type — Technical (coding), Case Study, or Behavioral — and start.


Question Generation
Questions are generated by Claude Sonnet, drawing from a 600+ question bank in Supabase and adapting to the target company and role. Technical questions always include sample data (SQL tables with column types, Python DataFrames with sample rows). Every question is scoped to be answerable in 2-3 minutes with only the provided data.
Quality rules are strict: one task per question, difficulty matched to JD seniority, company-specific tailoring (references to real products, never competitors), and a hardcoded skills allowlist so only genuinely codeable skills appear in technical mode.


Answer Evaluation
Answers are evaluated by Claude Sonnet (temperature 0) with structured feedback. Technical answers get correctness scoring. Behavioral answers are evaluated against STAR framework criteria. Feedback streams in real-time with a score, coaching notes, and specific suggestions.


Progress Tracking
The app tracks skill readiness across sessions using a multiplicative formula (score quality x practice volume). A skills readiness dashboard shows per-skill bars, score trends over time, best answers, and weak areas with targeted practice links. Gap analysis compares your resume against a JD to identify skill matches, gaps, and talking points.

title: InterviewReady
text:
Architecture

Frontend
Next.js 16 with App Router, React 19, Tailwind + shadcn/ui, and Zustand 5 for state management. Dark/light mode. Mobile-responsive with icon-only action bars and 44px tap targets on mobile.


Backend
All AI calls are server-side (no API keys exposed to the client). Three model tiers: Claude Sonnet for question generation and answer evaluation, Claude Haiku for hints, clarification, and JD parsing. Groq for voice transcription.


Auth & Data
Supabase Auth (Google OAuth + email magic link) with row-level security on all tables. Guest mode with cookie-based bypass for trying the app without an account. All 10 API routes validate authentication and input (Zod schemas). Rate limiting at 50 requests/hour per IP.


Security
Security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, nosniff), SSRF protection on URL fetching (blocks private IPs/localhost), auth callback redirect validation, and userId ownership checks on all data access.

title: InterviewReady
text:
What I’d Do Differently

Question classifier — Collecting thumbs up/down feedback now to train an XGBoost model that auto-filters low-quality generated questions
User-based rate limiting — Currently IP-based, which is rough for shared networks
Longer function timeouts — Vercel Hobby plan caps at 10 seconds, which occasionally truncates complex evaluations

title: InterviewReady
text:
Stack
Next.js | React | TypeScript | Tailwind | shadcn/ui | Zustand | Claude API | Supabase | Vercel

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text: View on GitHub

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
What This Project Does
Imagine you’re running marketing at a B2B software company. You have 100,000 accounts in your CRM — companies that might buy your product. But your team can only actively engage with maybe a few thousand of them this quarter. How do you decide which ones?
This project builds two tools to answer that question:

A propensity model that scores every account from 0 to 100% based on how likely they are to close a deal
A buying group analyzer that looks at accounts that should be closing but aren’t, figures out what’s missing (the right people aren’t involved), and recommends exactly who marketing should go find

Together, they turn a chaotic spreadsheet of accounts into a prioritized action plan.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: Why This Matters in the Real World
text:
Why This Matters in the Real World
At Atlassian, I did similar work — analyzing which contact profiles showed up on deals that closed, identifying accounts with coverage gaps, and building enrichment strategies that informed marketing’s contact acquisition priorities.
This project recreates that methodology on synthetic data so I can walk through the full approach openly, from data generation to model evaluation to strategic recommendations.
A note on synthetic data: The data generator embeds known signals at controlled strengths — for example, VP+ contacts on a deal increase win probability. The model’s job is to recover those signals from the feature matrix, validating that the pipeline works end-to-end. This demonstrates methodology, not empirical discovery. The same pipeline on real CRM data would face additional noise, confounders, and temporal dynamics — but the engineering and evaluation framework would carry over directly.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: The Data: What a CRM Looks Like
text:
The Data: What a CRM Looks Like
Before we get into the model, it helps to understand what data B2B marketing teams actually work with. The project generates synthetic data across four tables that mirror what you’d find in a real CRM (Customer Relationship Management) system like Salesforce:

Accounts (100,000 rows)
Each row is a company. We know their industry (Technology, Healthcare, etc.), size (employee count, annual revenue), segment (SMB, Mid-Market, or Enterprise), and tech stack — which software tools they already use. We also know if they’re already a customer of ours.


Contacts (717,820 rows)
Each row is a person at one of those companies. We know their job title, job function (Engineering, Marketing, Sales, etc.), seniority (from Individual Contributor up to C-Suite), and how they entered our system (paid search, organic, events, etc.).


Opportunities (50,000 rows)
Each row is a potential deal. It’s tied to an account and has a stage (Discovery → Evaluation → Proposal → Negotiation → Closed Won or Closed Lost), a dollar amount, and an outcome — did we win or lose?


Contact-Opportunity Bridge (122,677 rows)
This is the crucial table. It tells us which people are involved in which deals, and what role they play: Champion (our internal advocate), Decision Maker (signs the check), Evaluator (tests the product), Influencer (shapes opinion), or Blocker (raises objections).

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: Feature Engineering: Turning Raw Data Into Signals
text:
Feature Engineering: Turning Raw Data Into Signals
Raw CRM data isn’t directly useful for a model. A company name or a job title string doesn’t mean anything mathematically. Feature engineering is the process of transforming raw data into numeric signals that a model can learn from.
We build features at three levels, from weakest to strongest:

Level 1: Firmographic & Technographic Features
“What kind of company is this?”

Segment encoding — We convert “SMB / Mid-Market / Enterprise” into three separate yes/no columns. Why not just use 1/2/3? Because the relationship isn’t linear — Mid-Market actually has the highest win rate, not Enterprise. Separate columns let the model learn each segment’s unique effect.
Log-transformed size — Employee count and revenue are heavily skewed (most companies are small, a few are enormous). A log transform compresses this range so the model doesn’t treat a 20,000-person company as 400x more important than a 50-person one. The insight is that going from 50 to 500 employees matters more than going from 5,000 to 5,500.
Tech stack signals — We parse the comma-separated list of tools and create two features: a count of “high-signal” tools (Jira, Salesforce, Slack — tools that suggest the company is in our ecosystem), plus individual flags for the top 10 tools.



Level 2: Contact Composition Features
“Who do we know at this company?”
This is where it gets interesting. We aggregate everyone we know at an account:

VP+ count — How many VP or C-Suite contacts do we have? Seniority correlates strongly with deal closure.
Function diversity — Are we talking to one department or five? Deals that involve multiple departments are harder to start but more likely to close.
Senior density — What fraction of our contacts are senior? An account where 3 of 10 contacts are VPs is different from one where 1 of 50 is.
Technical + Business mix — Do we have contacts from both the “builder” side (Engineering, IT, Product) AND the “buyer” side (Marketing, Sales, Finance)? Deals close when both sides are at the table.



Level 3: Buying Group Features
“Who’s actively involved in the sales process?”
The strongest signals come from the deal contact data:

Deal contact count — How many distinct people are engaged on active deals?
Role coverage — Of the four key roles (Champion, Decision Maker, Evaluator, Influencer), how many are present?
Buying group completeness — A composite 0-100 score combining role coverage, seniority presence, function diversity, and technical/business mix

In total, we generate 39 features from these three levels.


A Design Decision: Avoiding Target Leakage
There’s a subtle but important problem here. The Level 3 features — deal contact count, role coverage, buying group completeness — are derived from the same opportunity records whose outcome is the target variable. If we include them in the propensity model, we’re letting the model peek at the answer.
The solution: the propensity model uses only Level 1 and Level 2 features (firmographic, technographic, and contact composition signals that exist before any deal starts). Level 3 features are reserved for the buying group analysis, where we’re describing deal patterns rather than predicting outcomes. This separation is enforced in the code with a include_deal_features flag.
This matters because many portfolio projects (and some production systems) accidentally leak future information into the training set. The result is a model that looks great in evaluation but fails in production, because it relied on information that wouldn’t be available at prediction time.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
The Model: Logistic Regression

What Logistic Regression Does
At its core, logistic regression answers a yes/no question: will this account close a deal? It takes all 39 features as inputs and outputs a probability between 0% and 100%.
Think of it like a weighted scorecard. Each feature gets a weight (called a coefficient). Positive weights increase the score; negative weights decrease it. The model learns these weights from historical data — it looks at hundreds of accounts that won or lost, and figures out which features are most predictive.
For example, if the model learns that “VP+ contacts on the deal” has a coefficient of +0.10, it means that having senior contacts engaged makes the account meaningfully more likely to close.


Why Logistic Regression Over Fancier Models
We also trained a Random Forest — a more complex model that builds hundreds of decision trees and averages their predictions. At 100K-account scale, it actually performed slightly worse than logistic regression (AUC 0.567 vs 0.575). We chose logistic regression because:

Interpretability: You can look at the coefficients and say “senior density is our strongest signal, existing customer status is second.” Try explaining a 100-tree ensemble to a marketing VP.
It’s what production systems use: Marketing platforms like Marketo and HubSpot use logistic regression under the hood for lead scoring. Building with the same approach makes the work directly transferable.
No performance justification for complexity: The simpler model matched or beat the fancier one, suggesting the signal structure is additive rather than interaction-driven.



What is Regularization?
Even with ~36K accounts and 39 features, there’s a risk of overfitting — the model memorizes quirks of the training data instead of learning real patterns. Regularization is a technique that penalizes large coefficients, essentially telling the model: “don’t put too much weight on any single feature.”
We use L2 regularization (also called Ridge), controlled by a parameter called C. Lower C = stronger regularization = simpler model. We tested C values of 0.01, 0.1, 1.0, and 10.0, and the model performed best at C=0.01 — fairly strong regularization that keeps feature redundancy (e.g., correlated size and revenue features) from inflating coefficients.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: How We Evaluate: Business Metrics That Matter
text:
How We Evaluate: Business Metrics That Matter
Technical metrics like accuracy aren’t very useful here. If 64% of accounts lose, a model that always predicts “lose” would be 64% accurate but completely useless. Instead, we evaluate with metrics that answer real business questions.

AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve)
What it is: A number between 0 and 1 that measures how well the model separates winners from losers. An AUC of 0.5 means the model is no better than flipping a coin. An AUC of 1.0 means perfect separation.
Our result: 0.575 (95% CI: 0.56–0.59) on the held-out test set (~7,200 accounts).
What that means: The model is meaningfully better than random. In practice, AUC values of 0.55-0.65 are common for account-level propensity models using only pre-deal features — the signal exists but it’s noisy, because many factors that determine whether a deal closes (budget timing, competitive dynamics, internal politics) aren’t in the data. The tight confidence interval (thanks to the large test set) gives us confidence this isn’t a fluke.


Does the Model Earn Its Complexity?
An AUC of 0.575 beats random, but “better than random” is a low bar. The more useful question: does the full 39-feature model beat what a marketing analyst could do with a single ORDER BY in SQL? To check, I compared it against four naive baselines on the same held-out test set, each using a progressively richer slice of the data. All confidence intervals are bootstrap estimates (1,000 resamples of the test set, 95% percentile interval), so they’re directly comparable.



Model
Features
Test AUC
95% CI




Random
0
0.494
0.480–0.507


Senior density (rank only)
1
0.548
0.535–0.562


Segment only
3
0.529
0.517–0.541


Firmographic only
30
0.562
0.549–0.575


Full model
39
0.575
0.561–0.589



Two things stand out. First, ranking accounts by one feature, senior contact density, gets you to 0.548, roughly half the gap from random to the full model. A team with no model at all could capture a meaningful chunk of the value with a single sort. Second, and more important: the full model’s confidence interval (0.561–0.589) overlaps the firmographic-only model’s (0.549–0.575). The nine contact-composition features add only about 0.013 AUC, and that lift is small enough that I can’t cleanly distinguish it from noise on this data.
The honest takeaway is that most of the pre-deal propensity signal is firmographic: who the company is matters more than the shape of the contact list we’ve assembled. The stronger signal from who is actually on the deal shows up later, in the buying group analysis, where those features describe deal patterns rather than predict outcomes. Including them in the propensity model would leak the answer, as covered above.


Precision @ K
What it is: If you only look at the top K% of model-scored accounts, what fraction actually won?
Our results:








Top K%
Precision
What it means




Top 10%
44.8%
Nearly half of the highest-scored accounts actually won (vs. 36% baseline)


Top 20%
44.2%
Precision holds well beyond the top decile


Top 30%
42.2%
Concentrated wins in the top tiers



Why it matters: This is the metric marketing cares about most. “If I can only engage 3,000 accounts this quarter out of 100,000, which ones?” The model says: pick the top-scored ones, and you’ll hit winners 45% of the time instead of 36%.


Lift Chart
What it is: We divide all accounts into 10 equal groups (deciles) based on their score, from highest to lowest. For each group, we measure how much more likely they are to win compared to the overall average. This ratio is called lift.
Our result: The top decile converts at 1.3x the baseline rate. Accounts in the bottom deciles convert well below baseline.
Why it matters: Lift tells you the concentration of value. If the top 30% of scored accounts capture a disproportionate share of wins, you can focus your team’s time there and ignore the rest.


Revenue Capture Curve
What it is: If you work through accounts from highest-scored to lowest, what percentage of total won revenue have you captured at each point?
Why it matters: It answers “how much money am I leaving on the table?” If the top 30% of scored accounts capture 50% of won revenue, the model is doing real work. If it’s close to 30% (what random selection would give you), the model isn’t helping.


Calibration
What it is: Are the predicted probabilities trustworthy? If the model says an account has a 40% chance of closing, does it actually close about 40% of the time?
Our result: Yes. Across score deciles, the largest gap between the predicted probability and the observed win rate is 2.4 percentage points. The Brier score (mean squared error of the probabilities) is 0.226, versus 0.230 for a no-skill model that always predicts the base rate.
What that means: This is the part of the model I trust most. The ranking is weak (AUC 0.575), but the probabilities themselves are honest: a predicted 40% really does win close to 40% of the time. That distinction matters operationally. If marketing sets a threshold like “work every account above 45%,” a well-calibrated score means the threshold behaves as expected, even though the model is not a strong separator overall.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: Feature Importance: What Drives the Score
text:
Feature Importance: What Drives the Score
The beauty of logistic regression is that you can look directly at the coefficients to see what matters. Since this is synthetic data with known signal structure, the model’s job is to recover the embedded signals — and the ranking confirms the pipeline does so correctly:
Top positive drivers (increase propensity):

Senior contact density — The fraction of VP+ contacts at an account is the single strongest signal. In real-world B2B sales, senior involvement is widely recognized as the strongest deal predictor.
Existing customer status — Upsell/cross-sell opportunities convert at meaningfully higher rates than net-new deals.
Complementary tech stack — Accounts that already use tools in our ecosystem (Jira, Salesforce, AWS) score higher.
Mid-Market segment — Mid-Market accounts convert at the highest rate, consistent with the “sweet spot” pattern common in B2B SaaS.

Top negative drivers (decrease propensity):

SMB segment and certain industries (Manufacturing, Education) — Smaller companies and industries with lower tech adoption close at lower rates.

Both the logistic regression and random forest models agreed on the top signals (senior density, tech stack, max seniority), which validates that the patterns are structural, not artifacts of one particular model type.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
Buying Group Analysis

What Is a Buying Group?
In B2B sales, purchasing decisions are rarely made by one person. A typical enterprise deal involves 4-7 people playing different roles:

Champion — Your internal advocate who pushes for the deal
Decision Maker — The person who signs the check (usually a VP or C-Suite exec)
Evaluator — The person who tests the product and assesses technical fit
Influencer — Someone whose opinion shapes the decision (could be a respected engineer, a trusted advisor)
Blocker — Someone who raises objections or slows the process

A deal is much more likely to close when all four positive roles are filled. If you’re missing a Champion, nobody’s pushing internally. If you’re missing a Decision Maker, there’s no one to sign off.


Completeness Scoring
We score each account’s buying group on a 0-100 scale across four dimensions (25 points each):








Dimension
What it measures
Full marks if…




Role coverage
Key buying roles present
All 4 roles filled


Seniority mix
Exec + mid-level engagement
Both VP+ and Director/Manager


Function diversity
Multiple departments involved
3+ distinct functions


Tech + Business
Builders and buyers both involved
Technical AND business contacts





The Results: Completeness Predicts Wins



Completeness Tier
Win Rate
Accounts




Complete (75-100)
49%
9,356


High (50-75)
39%
10,467


Medium (25-50)
28%
11,029


Low (0-25)
22%
5,121



Accounts with complete buying groups win at 2.2x the rate of those with low completeness — and this pattern holds across thousands of accounts per tier. This isn’t surprising — it’s hard to close a deal when the right people aren’t at the table — but the magnitude and consistency of the difference gives marketing a clear mandate: invest in filling coverage gaps.
A caution on how to read this: it’s an association, not a causal estimate. Complete buying groups tend to show up at larger, more engaged, later-stage accounts that were more likely to win anyway, so the raw 2.2x overstates the lift a team would get purely from filling gaps. Pinning down the actual causal effect would take a controlled test, randomly enriching some accounts and not others, or at minimum adjusting for account size, segment, and deal stage. The completeness score is still useful as a prioritization signal. It just shouldn’t be read as “fill the gap and win 2.2x more often.”


Gap Analysis: What’s Missing and What to Do About It
The analysis identified 29,512 accounts with specific coverage gaps. The most common gaps:

Missing buying roles (Champions and Decision Makers are the most commonly absent)
No VP+ contact engaged on the deal
Only one department represented (usually the team that initiated the conversation)

For each gap account, the system generates a specific enrichment recommendation: “This account needs a Champion in Engineering” or “No VP+ contact — target Director or VP-level outreach.”


Estimating Pipeline Value
The final step is quantifying the opportunity. The framework estimates pipeline value under explicit assumptions: a deal size of ~2% of account annual revenue, and a 10 percentage-point win rate uplift from filling coverage gaps.
The absolute dollar estimates from synthetic data are illustrative — in production, both assumptions would need calibration against real historical conversion data, and actual deal-to-revenue ratios and enrichment uplift vary significantly by segment, industry, and sales motion. The value of this step isn’t the specific number — it’s the framework for sizing the opportunity, which translates the analysis into a business case that executives can evaluate and pressure-test with their own assumptions.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
The Targeting Framework
The most powerful view combines both layers — propensity scores and buying group completeness — into a 2×2 framework:









Complete Buying Group
Gaps Exist




High Propensity
Close now — sales should be all over these
Enrich & close — highest-ROI enrichment targets


Low Propensity
Nurture — engaged but signals are weak
Low priority — gaps AND weak signals



The upper-right quadrant is the sweet spot: 7,068 accounts the model thinks are likely to buy, but where the buying group isn’t complete (30% win rate vs. 48% for high-propensity accounts with complete groups). These are the accounts where a targeted contact addition — finding the missing Champion, engaging the VP — could tip the deal.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
section: What I’d Do Differently in Production
text:
What I’d Do Differently in Production
This project uses synthetic data and runs offline. In a production environment, several things would change:

Title normalization — Real job titles are messy (“Sr. Dir. of Eng” vs “Senior Director, Engineering”). I’d use fuzzy matching or an LLM to standardize before feature extraction.
Temporal validation — Instead of random train/test splits, I’d train on earlier time periods and test on later ones. This better simulates how the model would perform on genuinely unseen future data.
Live scoring pipeline — Integrate with Salesforce or HubSpot via scheduled batch scoring, so scores update automatically as new contacts and deal activity come in.
Model monitoring — Track AUC and calibration over time, with alerts when performance drops. Retrain quarterly or when the product/market changes significantly.
A/B test the model — Before full rollout, randomly assign accounts to model-scored vs. control groups and measure the incremental lift in pipeline and bookings. This is the gold standard for proving the model actually drives results, not just correlates with them.

title: Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification
text:
Stack
Python | Pandas | scikit-learn | Matplotlib | Plotly | Streamlit | Jupyter

title: AI Search Visibility Scorecard
text: Live App

title: AI Search Visibility Scorecard
text:
What This Project Does
AI assistants now answer questions like “what’s the best PDF editor?” directly, and brands have no Search Console for that channel: no impression counts, no rank tracking, and answers that change every time you ask. This project is a working measurement framework for brand visibility in AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Google AI Overviews, Gemini, Perplexity, Copilot), built as the weekly scorecard a growth marketing analytics team would actually run.
It tracks five Adobe products against their competitive categories (Adobe Express vs Canva, Firefly vs Midjourney, Acrobat vs Smallpdf, and so on) on fully simulated data. The dataset is fake by design and labeled as such on every page; the measurement methodology is real and cited to published industry practice.

title: AI Search Visibility Scorecard
text:
The Measurement Problem
Traditional search measurement assumes a ranked list of links and a click you can attribute. Answer engines break all three assumptions:

No impressions or ranks. The engine synthesizes one answer; either your brand is in it or it isn’t, and nobody reports how often it was shown.
Stochastic answers. The same prompt returns a different brand list almost every run. Published research found the same brand list appears in fewer than 1 in 100 repeated runs, so a single check of “are we in the answer” is meaningless.
Broken attribution. Most AI-influenced visits lose their referrer on the way to your site. Clicks from Google AI Overviews arrive looking like ordinary Google traffic.

So the framework treats visibility like a survey instead of a log: sample repeatedly, estimate with uncertainty, and connect to business outcomes through stated assumptions rather than pretending click-path attribution exists.

title: AI Search Visibility Scorecard
text:
How It Works

The prompt panel
The unit of measurement is a fixed panel of 40 prompts across 5 product categories, tagged by intent stage (discovery, evaluation, how-to, suitability) and weighted by estimated ask volume. Each prompt is re-asked many times per engine per week, mirroring how production tools like Adobe LLM Optimizer license tracked prompts.


Sampling with honest uncertainty
Mention rates are binomial estimates from 70 simulated runs per prompt, engine, and week, and every rate carries a Wilson 95% confidence interval. The interval is the point: 16 mentions in 20 runs reads as 80%, but the honest range is 58% to 92%. Weekly moves inside the interval are reported as noise, not news.


The metric set
Mention rate, share of voice against tracked competitors, average position in the answer, citation share (whether your domain is the cited source, which is a different event from being named), sentiment conditional on mention, and a composite Visibility Score with openly stated weights. The vocabulary deliberately matches Adobe LLM Optimizer’s published terms so the demo speaks the language of the production tool category.


From visibility to revenue
The Outcomes page builds a proxy chain from visibility to sessions to signups to an annualized new-revenue figure, with every assumption exposed as a slider and a sensitivity table showing which assumption moves the answer most. It also models the dark-traffic gap: the difference between what an analytics referrer report would credit and the modeled true AI-influenced traffic.


Experiments without a clean ground truth
You cannot randomize what ChatGPT says, so the experiment design uses treated-vs-control prompt panels with pooled pre/post reads. The demo includes one content experiment with a clear lift against a flat control, and one PR experiment with an honestly reported null on one engine. The null is presented as a feature of the process: it narrows the playbook.


Methodology page
The centerpiece for anyone evaluating the work: metric formulas, panel design, ten documented limitations (dark traffic, panel representativeness, model-version churn, vendor self-reporting), full data provenance, and references to the published research the framework is grounded in.

title: AI Search Visibility Scorecard
text:
Data Honesty
All numbers in the app are generated by a seeded, deterministic script with binomial sampling at the prompt level, so confidence intervals and experiment reads behave the way real sampled data would. No real answer engines were scraped, no Adobe data was used, and nothing in the app is a claim about real brand performance, including the competitor gaps. This is an independent portfolio project, not affiliated with or endorsed by Adobe; product names are used only to make the measurement framework concrete.

title: AI Search Visibility Scorecard
text:
What I’d Do Differently

Real sampling pipeline. Swap the simulated sampler for scheduled runs against engine APIs with the same statistics. The framework transfers; the plumbing changes.
Demand-validated panel. A fixed prompt list under-samples how differently real users phrase the same intent; the panel should be validated against real query and prompt-volume data.
Outcome-calibrated weights. The Visibility Score weights are stated demo choices. The production version should calibrate them against what actually predicts referred sessions and signups.

title: AI Search Visibility Scorecard
text:
Stack
Next.js | React | TypeScript | Tailwind | Recharts | Vercel

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text: View on GitHub

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
The Problem
Data teams answer the same business questions differently depending on who’s asking. A VP of Sales wants pipeline forecasts with revenue implications. A product manager wants user engagement metrics with cohort breakdowns. The underlying data is the same — the context is what changes.
Adaptive Analyst Agent is an AI-powered data analyst that automatically adapts its analysis style, metric selection, and communication approach based on who it’s talking to.

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
How It Works
The agent uses Claude’s tool use capabilities to:

Load stakeholder context — Markdown profiles defining each audience’s priorities, KPIs, and communication preferences
Query data — Execute SQL against a DuckDB database with synthetic GTM data
Adapt the analysis — Frame results, choose visualizations, and adjust technical depth based on the active stakeholder context


Architecture

Agent loop — Claude API with tool use for multi-step reasoning
Context system — Configurable markdown files (stakeholder profiles, domain knowledge, analysis frameworks)
Execution layer — Sandboxed SQL and Python execution via DuckDB
UI — Streamlit app for interactive exploration

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
In Action
Same question, different stakeholder — the agent adapts automatically:

Executive asks “How is pipeline trending?” → Board-level summary: total pipeline value, LTV/CAC ratio, YoY comparisons, and a one-line assessment of whether the quarter is on track.
Marketing asks “How is pipeline trending?” → Campaign-level breakdown: channel attribution, cost per MQL by source, specific winners and losers, and recommendations for budget reallocation.

The underlying SQL queries overlap significantly. What changes is metric selection, aggregation level, and how the results are framed.

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
Evaluation
Most “chat with your data” demos look impressive but never show whether the agent actually gets the answer right. I built a two-part evaluation so this one does.

Gold set: 20 well-specified questions, deterministic scoring
Each question has reference SQL whose result against the live DuckDB is the canonical answer. The runner extracts the agent’s numeric answer from a tagged response, compares with a per-question tolerance (exact for counts, percentage-point for rates, relative for dollar amounts), and reports a pass rate. The agent passed all 20 on the first run.
That number proves the agent writes correct SQL for well-specified questions on this schema. It does not prove the agent does the right thing when a question is ambiguous, when the data doesn’t support an answer, or when the obvious SQL approach is wrong.


Adversarial set: 12 questions designed to break things, LLM-judged
A Haiku judge scores each response against a PASS/FAIL rubric. The categories are deliberate:

Hallucination refusal. “What’s our NPS by segment?” The data has no NPS. Does the agent refuse, or fabricate?
Ambiguous metric. “What’s our conversion rate?” Lead-to-MQL? MQL-to-close? Does the agent disambiguate, or pick one silently?
SQL trap. “How many opportunities are open?” The obvious filter is_won = FALSE wrongly includes Closed Lost. Does the agent reach for the right column?
Leading question. “Why is Technology our best industry?” It’s not. Media is. Does the agent verify the premise, or play along?

The agent scored 7 of 12. The failures were the point. It fabricated a “churn rate” from closed-lost deals with the disclaimer buried in a footnote. It reported segments as if they were subscription tiers. It returned a number for “opportunities closed in 2025” without saying whether it meant calendar year or fiscal year. The SQL traps and the leading-question check, it handled correctly.
A 58% pass rate is not a finished product. It is a measured product. The repo includes the question sets, the judge, and the JSON of every run, so anyone reviewing can see exactly which questions failed and why.

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
Key Design Choices

Tool use over RAG — Structured data questions benefit from live SQL queries, not pre-embedded answers. The agent needs to act (run queries, generate charts), not just retrieve static content.
Read-only database as primary security layer — The agent executes arbitrary SQL, so the database connection is read-only by design. No risk of accidental writes or deletions regardless of what the LLM generates.
Structured context loading, not RAG — The project uses structured context loading — stakeholder profiles and domain knowledge loaded into the system prompt — not retrieval from a vector store. It’s technically closer to context injection than RAG. The design choice was intentional: for a small corpus of stakeholder profiles, full context loading is simpler and more reliable than embedding-based retrieval.
DuckDB over SQLite — Columnar storage is better suited for analytical queries. Supports window functions and CTEs natively.
Markdown for context — Easier to read, version, and customize than YAML or JSON. Stakeholder profiles read like documentation.

title: Adaptive Analyst Agent
text:
Stack
Python | Claude API | DuckDB | Streamlit | Pandas | Plotly

title: Job Title Normalizer
text: View on GitHub

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
What This Project Does
CRM databases are full of job titles that look like they were typed by someone in a hurry — because they were. You get “Sr. Dir. Mktg (EMEA)”, “SENIOR DIRECTOR ENGINEERING”, “Snr. Direcotr, Engg” — all describing the same kind of person but completely different as text. When you need to segment contacts by seniority or function — which is the first step in building buying groups, scoring leads, or targeting campaigns — this inconsistency makes everything downstream unreliable.
This project trains a neural network to classify raw, messy titles into two standardized labels simultaneously:

Seniority — one of 10 levels, from Individual Contributor through C-Suite
Function — one of 10 categories: Engineering, Data, Product, Marketing, Sales, Finance, HR, Operations, Design, Legal

The model predicts both at once through a shared representation, which is more efficient and more accurate than building two separate classifiers.

title: Job Title Normalizer
section: Why This Matters in the Real World
text:
Why This Matters in the Real World
At Atlassian, I built contact segmentation and buying group analysis for enterprise marketing. The prerequisite to all of that work was normalizing job titles — you can’t identify coverage gaps in a buying group if you don’t know who’s a VP vs. an IC, or who’s in engineering vs. marketing. Title normalization was the critical first step.
This project isolates that step as a standalone system. The seniority and function labels it produces are exactly the features that would feed into the lead scoring and buying group models in a production pipeline.

title: Job Title Normalizer
section: The Data: Why CRM Noise Is Harder Than It Looks
text:
The Data: Why CRM Noise Is Harder Than It Looks
Instead of using clean, synthetic data that would make the model look artificially good, the data generator mirrors what real CRM exports actually look like. There are 15 categories of noise, all drawn from real-world data quality issues:
The obvious stuff — abbreviations (Sr., Dir., Mgr.), case variation (UPPERCASE, lowercase, Title Case), extra whitespace, and character-swap typos.
The stuff that actually makes CRM data painful:

Truncation — CRM fields have character limits. “Senior Vice President of Engineering” becomes “Senior Vice Presi…” and now you’re missing the critical information
Geographic qualifiers — Someone appended “(EMEA)” or “- North America” because that’s what their admin system required
Credential suffixes — “MBA”, “CPA”, “PMP” stuck on the end because people copy their LinkedIn bio into the title field
Misspellings — Not just swapped characters, but real mistakes: “Manger” for Manager, “Engineeer” with a triple e, “Marekting” for Marketing
Word reordering — “Director of Engineering” vs “Engineering, Director” — different CRM systems format differently
Encoding artifacts — Smart quotes, em dashes, HTML entities, non-breaking spaces from system imports
Word drops — Lazy data entry where someone typed “VP” instead of “VP of Engineering”

This noise is layered stochastically — each title gets a random subset — so the model has to generalize to combinations it hasn’t seen.
An important limitation: All noise is applied to a fixed set of ~500 canonical title templates across 10 functions. The model never encounters a title structure it hasn’t seen some variant of. Real CRM data includes titles outside any taxonomy — “Chief Evangelist,” “Growth Hacker,” “Sustainability Officer” — plus non-English titles and hybrid roles like “Marketing Engineer” that blend functions. This synthetic setup validates the pipeline and architecture, but production accuracy would likely drop 5-10 points until the model is retrained on real labeled data.

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
The Models: Why Multi-Task Learning

What Multi-Task Learning Is
Instead of training one model for seniority and a separate one for function, we train one model that predicts both simultaneously. The key insight is that seniority and function share structure. “CTO” tells you both seniority (C-Suite) and function (Engineering) at once. “Manager” tells you seniority but not function. By sharing internal representations, the model learns this structure more efficiently.
The architecture has three parts:

Word embeddings — Each token in a title (after tokenization) maps to a 64-dimensional vector. During training, the model learns that “VP”, “V.P.”, and “Vice President” should all have similar vectors
Shared encoder — Processes the embedded title into a fixed-size representation. This is where seniority and function signals merge
Task-specific heads — Two separate linear layers, one for each prediction



Two Encoder Architectures
BiLSTM reads the title as a sequence, left-to-right and right-to-left, maintaining memory of what it’s seen. The bidirectional pass means “VP of Engineering” and “Engineering VP” produce similar representations.
CNN slides filters across the title looking for local patterns — 2-word, 3-word, and 4-word combinations like “VP of”, “Director of Engineering”, or “Sr. Dir.” It’s faster and slightly more accurate here because title classification depends on local n-gram patterns more than long-range dependencies.


The Baseline: Know What Simple Gets You
Before going neural, we trained TF-IDF + Logistic Regression — the simplest reasonable approach. TF-IDF converts titles to numeric vectors by counting weighted word frequencies. Logistic Regression draws a decision boundary. This establishes a performance floor: if the neural network can’t beat this, it’s not worth the complexity.

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
Results



Model
Seniority Acc
Function Acc
Both Correct




CNN (PyTorch)
95.2%
93.0%
89.0%


BiLSTM (PyTorch)
94.5%
92.6%
88.0%


TF-IDF + LogReg (baseline)
93.3%
93.3%
87.6%



20,000 synthetic titles total, split 70/15/15: 14,000 train, 3,000 validation (for early stopping), 3,000 held-out test.
Why these numbers are realistic, not inflated: The accuracy is in the high 80s to mid 90s — not 99%. That’s by design. The synthetic noise is calibrated to be aggressive, and the model genuinely struggles with ambiguous cases. “Senior” vs “Lead” seniority is hard when the title is truncated. “HR” vs “Operations” is hard when the title says “People Operations Manager.” Real CRM data would likely push combined accuracy down another 5-10 points, as it introduces title structures and vocabulary the model has never seen.
Honestly assessing the neural network’s value: The gap over TF-IDF (89.0% vs 87.6% combined) is 1.4 percentage points. That’s real but small — and it comes with real costs: PyTorch dependencies, embedding layers, training loops, and GPU for scale. TF-IDF + LogReg trains in seconds, needs no GPU, and is trivially deployable.
So when is the neural network worth it? At scale. On a database of 100K contacts, 1.4 percentage points is ~1,400 additional correct classifications. The gap is concentrated in the hardest cases — abbreviated titles, misspellings, and reordered titles where learned embeddings capture similarity that bag-of-words misses. In production, I’d start with TF-IDF as a fast baseline and upgrade to the CNN only if the error cases at the margin are costing downstream analysis quality.

Per-Class Performance
The model performs best on classes with distinctive vocabulary:

C-Suite — near-perfect (CTO, CFO, CMO are unambiguous)
Legal — distinctive terms (Counsel, Paralegal, Compliance)
Design — UX/UI vocabulary is specific

It struggles most where vocabulary overlaps:

Individual Contributor vs Senior — when seniority markers get truncated or misspelled, the model loses the only signal distinguishing the two
HR vs Operations — “People Operations” is an HR function despite the word “Operations”
Operations vs Engineering — In real CRM data, “DevOps” sits at the boundary between these functions. In this synthetic dataset, DevOps titles are consistently labeled under Engineering, so the model learns them cleanly — but this is a taxonomy decision that different companies would make differently.



Taxonomy Decisions Worth Noting
Two deliberate choices in the classification schema:
“Product Manager” is labeled as Individual Contributor. This is counterintuitive — the title contains “Manager.” But in most tech companies, “Product Manager” is a function descriptor, not a seniority marker. A PM is an IC role; the people-management equivalent is “Director of Product” or “VP of Product.” This is the exact kind of taxonomy ambiguity that makes title normalization hard in practice — “Manager” means different things depending on context.
Some seniority/function combinations don’t exist in the training data. Functions like Design and Legal don’t have SVP-level templates, because those titles are rare in practice. This means the model has never seen “SVP of Design” and would misclassify it. In production, this would be caught by confidence monitoring — the model would output a low-confidence prediction, flagging it for human review.

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
Training: How the Model Learns

Early Stopping
The model trains for up to 30 passes through the data (epochs). After each pass, we check performance on held-out data. If performance stops improving for 5 consecutive epochs (patience), training halts and we revert to the best checkpoint. This prevents the model from memorizing the training data — a problem called overfitting that’s especially dangerous with 20,000 samples.


What the Model Learns
During training, the embedding layer discovers semantic structure: abbreviations cluster with their full forms, seniority markers group together, function-specific terms form distinct neighborhoods. This learned vocabulary is the main advantage over TF-IDF, which treats every distinct string as a completely independent feature.

title: Job Title Normalizer
section: How This Connects to the Lead Scoring Pipeline
text:
How This Connects to the Lead Scoring Pipeline
Title normalization is Step 1 in a contact data pipeline:

Normalize titles → standardized seniority and function labels (this project)
Build contact features → VP+ count, function diversity, senior density per account
Score accounts → propensity model using contact features + firmographics
Identify gaps → buying group completeness analysis

Without reliable title normalization, everything downstream is built on noise. If “Sr. Dir. Mktg” doesn’t get classified as a senior director in marketing, the buying group analysis doesn’t know there’s a senior marketing leader on the account, and the gap analysis might incorrectly recommend finding one.

title: Job Title Normalizer
section: What I’d Do Differently in Production
text:
What I’d Do Differently in Production

Real labeled data — Retrain on titles from actual CRM exports (Salesforce, HubSpot). The synthetic generator gets you 80% of the way, but production titles have patterns no generator anticipates
Active learning — Flag low-confidence predictions for human review. A model that says “60% engineering, 35% data” is asking for help — route those to a human and use the corrections to retrain
Fine-tuned LLM — For the hardest 10% of titles (creative titles, non-English, multi-role), a fine-tuned language model would capture context that word embeddings miss
Monitoring — Track prediction confidence distributions over time. If a new client uses unusual title conventions, confidence will drop and you’ll know to retrain

title: Job Title Normalizer
text:
Stack
Python | PyTorch | scikit-learn | Pandas | NumPy | Matplotlib | pytest

title: GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly
text: View on GitHub Live docs + lineage

title: GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly
text:
What This Project Does
Every company’s go-to-market data starts out messy. The same customer is entered twice under different IDs. The same person shows up under three email addresses. Marketing events arrive keyed on email while the CRM is keyed on a contact ID, so a naive join quietly counts the same touch five times. Analysts build dashboards on top of all this and wonder why the numbers never quite tie out.
Analytics engineering is the job of fixing that once, in one place, so nobody downstream has to. This project is a full analytics-engineering warehouse built with dbt: it takes six raw, deliberately messy source tables and turns them into clean, tested, documented tables that a revenue team can actually trust. It runs end to end on a free local database with one command, and every model is documented with an interactive lineage graph you can click through in the live docs.

title: GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly
section: Why This Matters in the Real World
text:
Why This Matters in the Real World
A data scientist’s model is only as good as the table it’s trained on. Most of the pain in a data team is not the model, it’s the layer underneath: the transformations that turn raw system exports into trustworthy facts and dimensions. That layer is what analytics engineers own, and it is exactly the work I did at Atlassian when I reconciled a large CRM dataset and fixed a join that was double-counting contacts.
This project recreates that discipline from scratch on a clean, public codebase, so the craft is visible: the layering, the tests, the documentation, and the data quality logic.






Note



A note on the data. The source tables are synthetic and generated with a fixed random seed, so every run produces identical output and no real customer data is involved. The dollar figures below are illustrative and exist only to exercise the models. What is real is the modeling, the tests, and the data-quality logic. The messiness in the data, duplicate companies, duplicate contacts, inconsistent formatting, and the email-keyed marketing join, is modeled on the kinds of problems that actually show up in B2B go-to-market data.

title: GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly
text:
How It’s Structured
dbt projects are built in layers, and each layer has one job. Raw data comes in at the top, gets cleaned, gets reshaped, and lands as tables that answer business questions.





flowchart TD
    A["Raw sources (6 tables, ~86k rows)<br/>CRM · marketing · product · web"] --> B["staging<br/>clean, cast, normalize keys"]
    B --> C["intermediate<br/>reconcile accounts · dedup contacts<br/>fix the marketing join · build the funnel"]
    C --> D["marts / core<br/>dim_accounts · dim_contacts<br/>fct_opportunities · fct_product_usage"]
    C --> E["marts / gtm<br/>account health · buying group<br/>lead-scoring features · pipeline funnel"]
    D --> F["semantic layer<br/>bookings · pipeline · win rate"]
    E --> G["consumers<br/>lead-scoring model · GTM dashboard"]

title: GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly
text:
Three Problems It Solves
I built the project around the three problems that actually make GTM data hard. These are the parts worth reading.
1. The same company, entered twice. The raw CRM has duplicate accounts that share a web domain, and duplicate contacts that share an email. Report on the raw IDs and you double-count companies and people. The warehouse picks one golden record per domain, collapses duplicate contacts, and a test fails the build if a duplicate ever slips through again. In this build, 408 raw account rows resolve to 400 real companies, and 3,035 raw contacts resolve to 2,826 people.
2. A join that double-counts, caught by a test. Marketing touches arrive keyed on email, not contact ID, and the CRM holds duplicate-email contacts. Join them naively and a single marketing touch matches several duplicate contacts and gets counted multiple times. The fix is to join against the deduplicated contact set, and a dedicated test asserts that the number of attributed touches exactly equals the number of source touches, so the join can never silently break again. This is the same class of bug as the join fix I worked on at Atlassian.
3. Cheap rebuilds and point-in-time history. The product usage table is built incrementally: a full run scans all 50,000 events, but a routine run only reprocesses the most recent days, so it stays cheap to refresh. And an account snapshot records a new versioned row whenever a company’s size or industry changes, so the warehouse can answer “what segment was this account in when the deal closed,” not just what it looks like today.

title: GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly
section: The Trust Layer: Tests, Docs, and CI
text:
The Trust Layer: Tests, Docs, and CI
A transformation is only useful if people trust it. This project treats that as a first-class requirement, not an afterthought:

57 data tests run on every build, from simple “this key is unique and not null” checks to custom business rules like “every domain resolves to exactly one account.”
100% documentation coverage, enforced by a script that fails the build if any model or column is left undocumented.
Continuous integration rebuilds the entire warehouse from scratch in a clean environment on every push, runs all the tests, checks documentation coverage, and confirms the metrics layer still answers a query. The badge on the repo is green because the whole thing genuinely reproduces, not because I said so.

title: GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly
text:
One Tasteful Use of AI
Writing column descriptions is the chore everyone skips, which is why so many data warehouses are undocumented. The project includes an optional tool that sends a model’s SQL to Claude and asks for first-draft descriptions, written to a review file rather than merged automatically. Documentation is reviewed, not auto-generated, and every description in the repo was written and edited by hand. The audit half of that same tool runs offline with no AI at all and is what gates the build. It is a small, honest use of AI: speed up the blank-page problem, without pretending the machine owns the documentation.

title: GTM Analytics Warehouse — A dbt Project Done Properly
text:
Stack
dbt · DuckDB · MetricFlow · SQL · Python · GitHub Actions

title: Philip Felix
text:
Philip Felix

Senior Data Scientist — SF Bay Area


A decade in analytics working with marketing, sales, customer success, and product teams: Product Specialist and Operations Analyst at Square, then Data Scientist at Atlassian for the last six years.



Projects




AI Search Visibility Scorecard


AI assistants now answer “what’s the best PDF editor?” directly, and there is no Search Console for that channel: no impressions, no ranks, and answers that change every run. A measurement framework for brand visibility in AI answer engines, built as a weekly scorecard: prompt-panel sampling with confidence intervals, share of voice against competitors, treated-vs-control experiments with an honestly reported null, and a proxy model connecting visibility to revenue. Simulated data, real methodology.


Next.js · TypeScript · Recharts · Statistics · Vercel




GTM Analytics Warehouse — dbt


Go-to-market data starts out messy: the same company entered twice, the same person under three emails, marketing events that double-count on a naive join. A full analytics-engineering warehouse built with dbt that fixes it once, in one place. Six raw source tables turned into clean, tested, documented models, with reconciliation logic, an incremental table, point-in-time snapshots, a metrics layer, and CI that rebuilds and tests the whole thing on every push.


dbt · DuckDB · MetricFlow · SQL · Python · GitHub Actions




EnrichEval — Transaction Enrichment Quality


Your banking app turns a cryptic string like SQ *BLUE BOTTLE COFFEE OAKLAND CA into a clean merchant, category, and recurring flag. Building that is easy; knowing how good it is, is hard. A harness that measures enrichment quality — defining what “correct” means, turning a vague complaint into a ranked list of fixes, validating an AI judge against human labels, and catching regressions when the model changes.


Python · pandas · scikit-learn · Streamlit · Claude API




Support Ticket Classification


Which classification method should you use — and how does the answer change based on how much labeled data you have? Seven methods compared from 100 to ~9.5K labeled tickets, from TF-IDF baselines to fine-tuned LLMs, on synthetic data modeled after a real AI-product support taxonomy.


Python · Next.js · TypeScript · Recharts




Adaptive Analyst Agent


An LLM-powered analyst that answers the same business question differently depending on who’s asking. A VP gets a board-level summary. A campaign manager gets channel attribution and budget recommendations. Same data, different context.


Python · Claude API · DuckDB · Streamlit




Lead Scoring & Buying Group Identification


You have 100K accounts and can only engage a few thousand this quarter. Which ones? A propensity model to answer that, plus a buying group analyzer that figures out why good accounts aren’t closing and who’s missing from the deal.


Python · Pandas · scikit-learn · Streamlit




Job Title Normalizer


Sales and marketing teams route deals and score leads off job titles, but CRM titles are chaos — abbreviations, regions, seniority markers, punctuation. A multi-task PyTorch classifier that normalizes them into clean seniority and function labels.


Python · PyTorch · scikit-learn
